Obedience must be, 1. Careful, paying attention to what you do; 2. Universal, doing all the commandments; and 3. From a good principle, with regard to God as the Lord, your God, and with a holy fear of him. This should motivate your obedience. Moses directs you to look back. It is good to remember all the ways, both of God's providence and grace, by which he has led us through this wilderness, that we may cheerfully serve him and trust in him. You must remember the difficult situations you were sometimes brought into, for mortifying your pride and manifesting your perverseness; to test you, so that you and others might know all that was in your heart, and that everyone might see that God chose you, not for anything in you which might recommend you to his favor. You must remember the miraculous supplies of food and raiment granted to you. Let none of God's children distrust their Father, nor take any sinful course for the supply of their necessities. Somehow, God will provide for them in the way of duty and honest diligence, and truly they will be fed. This may be applied spiritually; the word of God is the food of the soul. Christ is the word of God; by him we live. You must also remember the rebukes you have been under, and not without need. This use we should make of all our afflictions; by them let us be quickened to our duty. Moses also directs you to look forward to Canaan. Look which way you will, both to look back and to look forward, to Canaan. Looking which way you will, both to look back and to look forward will furnish you with arguments for obedience. Moses saw in that land a type of the better country. The gospel church is the New Testament Canaan, watered with the Spirit in his gifts and graces, planted with trees of righteousness, bearing fruits of righteousness. Heaven is the good land, in which nothing is wanting, and where there is fullness of joy.

Moses directs to the duty of a prosperous condition. Let you always remember your Benefactor. In everything we must give thanks. Moses arms you against the temptations of a prosperous condition. When people possess large estates, or are engaged in profitable business, they find the temptation to pride, forgetfulness of God, and carnal-mindedness very strong; and they are anxious and troubled about many things. In this, the believing poor have an advantage; they more easily perceive their supplies coming from the Lord in answer to the prayer of faith; and, strange as it may seem, they find less difficulty in simply trusting him for daily bread. They taste a sweetness therein, which is generally unknown to the rich, while they are also freed from many of their temptations. Do not forget God's former dealings with you. Here is the great secret of Divine Providence. Infinite wisdom and goodness are the source of all the changes and trials believers experience. Israel had many bitter trials, but it was “to do them good.” Pride is natural to the human heart. Would one suppose that such a people, after their slavery at the brick-kilns, should need the thorns of the wilderness to humble them? But such is man! And they were tested that they might be humbled. None of us live a single week without giving proofs of our weakness, folly, and depravity. To broken-hearted souls alone the Savior is precious indeed. Nothing can render the most suitable outward and inward trials effectual, but the power of the Spirit of God. See here how God's giving and our getting are reconciled, and apply it to spiritual wealth. All God's gifts are in pursuance of his promises. Moses repeats the warning he had often given of the fatal consequences of forsaking God. Those who follow others in sin, will follow them to destruction. If you do as sinners do, you must expect to fare as sinners fare.
